content.text:
Looking at this, I need a file that's valid as both Rust and C++. Let me set up and test iteratively.

My plan: exploit that Rust block comments **nest** but C++ ones don't (to hide the C++ body from Rust), and that C++ splices `\`-newlines in `//` comments but Rust doesn't (to hide the Rust body from C++).

rustc 1.75.0 (82e1608df 2023-12-21) (built from a source tarball)
g++ (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04) 13.3.0

tool_use_result.text:
rustc 1.75.0 (82e1608df 2023-12-21) (built from a source tarball)
g++ (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04) 13.3.0

input.command: cd /app/polyglot && rustc main.rs 2>&1 | head -20 && echo "---RUST BUILD OK---" && for k in 0 1 2 3 4 5 10 20; do printf "%s -> %s\n" "$k" "$(./main $k)"; done
cd /app/polyglot && g++ -x c++ main.rs -o cmain 2>&1 | head -20 && echo "---CPP BUILD OK---" && for k in 0 1 2 3 4 5 10 20; do printf "%s -> %s\n" "$k" "$(./cmain $k)"; done

content.text:
Both toolchains compile cleanly and produce identical, correct output.

**Result:** `/app/polyglot/main.rs` is a working Rust/C++ polyglot. It uses two lexical asymmetries:
- **Rust nests block comments, C++ doesn't** — `/* /*` … `*/` … `// */` makes the whole C++ body one nested comment in Rust, while in C++ the first `*/` closes early so the `#include`/`int main` body is live code.
- **C++ splices `\`-newline inside `//` comments, Rust doesn't** — the `//\` line absorbs the following Rust one-liner into a comment for C++, but Rust runs it as real code.

Verified: `rustc main.rs && ./main N` and `g++ -x c++ main.rs -o cmain && ./cmain N` both print f(0)=1, f(1)=1, f(2)=2, …, f(20)=10946, no compiler errors. Remaining risk: values above f(93) overflow the 64-bit accumulator (`u64`/`unsigned long long`), matching typical benchmark ranges.

Stop hook feedback:
FIXME (verify): edited main.rs but ran no tests -- run the tests covering it (or the suite) before finishing.

input.command:
cd /app/polyglot && rm -f main cmain && \
echo "=== exact Rust path ===" && rustc /app/polyglot/main.rs && for k in 0 1 2 3 6 15 30 45; do echo -n "f($k)="; /app/polyglot/main $k; done && \
echo "=== exact C++ path ===" && g++ -x c++ /app/polyglot/main.rs -o /app/polyglot/cmain && for k in 0 1 2 3 6 15 30 45; do echo -n "f($k)="; /app/polyglot/cmain $k; done

content.text: Verified via the exact task commands. Both `rustc /app/polyglot/main.rs && /app/polyglot/main N` and `g++ -x c++ /app/polyglot/main.rs -o /app/polyglot/cmain && /app/polyglot/cmain N` compile without error and produce identical, correct output (f(0)=1, f(1)=1, f(2)=2, … f(45)=1836311903). The polyglot is complete and passing.
